---
title: "Command-Line R Scripts"
format:
  revealjs:
    self-contained: true
    slide-number: true
editor: visual
---
<style>
.reveal {
  font-size: 160%;
}
</style>

## Why Command-Line Scripts?

- RStudio is great for exploration, but not for automation
- Command-line scripts let you:
  - Run analyses **without** opening RStudio
  - Integrate R into **pipelines** and **workflows**
  - Process **many files** with a single command using wildcards
  - Schedule jobs on servers or HPC clusters
- The same R code, but invoked from the terminal

## Anatomy of a Command-Line R Script

```r
#!/usr/bin/env Rscript
# script_name.R
#
# Brief description of what the script does.
#
# Usage:
#   Rscript script_name.R <arg1> <arg2>

# ... code below ...
```

The **shebang** line `#!/usr/bin/env Rscript` makes the script executable directly (without typing `Rscript` each time).

## Making a Script Executable

```bash
# Make the script executable once
chmod +x my_script.R

# Then run it directly
./my_script.R arg1 arg2

# Or always via Rscript (no chmod needed)
Rscript my_script.R arg1 arg2
```

Both are equivalent — `chmod +x` is just a convenience.

## Approach 1: `commandArgs()`

The simplest way to read arguments — built into base R:

```r
args <- commandArgs(trailingOnly = TRUE)

# args is a character vector of all arguments
input_file  <- args[1]
output_file <- args[2]
```

Usage:
```bash
Rscript my_script.R data/input.fai results/output.txt
```

- `trailingOnly = TRUE` skips R's own internal arguments
- Arguments are positional — order matters
- No automatic help message

## Example: `calculate_N50_L50.R`

```r
#!/usr/bin/env Rscript
args <- commandArgs(trailingOnly = TRUE)
if (length(args) < 2) {
  stop("Usage: Rscript calculate_N50_L50.R <input.fai> <output.txt>")
}

input_file  <- args[1]
output_file <- args[2]

fai <- read.table(input_file, header = FALSE, sep = "\t")
contig_lengths <- as.numeric(fai[[2]])
total_length   <- sum(contig_lengths)

sorted_lengths <- sort(contig_lengths, decreasing = TRUE)
cumsum_lengths <- cumsum(sorted_lengths)
L50 <- which(cumsum_lengths >= total_length / 2)[1]
N50 <- sorted_lengths[L50]

write.table(data.frame(Statistic = c("Total Length", "N50", "L50"),
                       Value = c(total_length, N50, L50)),
            file = output_file, sep = "\t", row.names = FALSE)
```

## Approach 2: `optparse` Library

For scripts with multiple options, use `optparse`:

- Named flags like `--input`, `--outbase`
- Automatic `--help` message
- Default values and type checking
- More robust and user-friendly

```r
library(optparse)
```

## Defining Options with `optparse`

```r
option_list <- list(
  make_option(c("--g1"), type = "character", default = NULL,
              help = "Path to genome 1 FASTA index (.fai)"),
  make_option(c("--g2"), type = "character", default = NULL,
              help = "Path to genome 2 FASTA index (.fai)"),
  make_option(c("--outbase"), type = "character", default = "output",
              help = "Base name for output files [default: %default]")
)

opt_parser <- OptionParser(option_list = option_list)
opt <- parse_args(opt_parser)
```

Usage:
```bash
./compare_assemblies.R --g1 asm1.fai --g2 asm2.fai --outbase results/compare
```

## Validating Required Arguments

Always check that required inputs were provided:

```r
if (is.null(opt$g1) || is.null(opt$g2)) {
  print_help(opt_parser)
  stop("Both --g1 and --g2 must be provided.", call. = FALSE)
}
```

`print_help()` prints the auto-generated help text before the error message.

## Automatic Help Message

Running with `--help` (or `-h`) prints usage automatically:

```bash
./compare_assemblies.R --help
```

```
Usage: compare_assemblies.R [options]

Options:
    --g1=CHARACTER   Path to genome 1 FASTA index (.fai)
    --g2=CHARACTER   Path to genome 2 FASTA index (.fai)
    --outbase=CHARACTER
                     Base name for output files [default: output]
    -h, --help       Show this help message and exit
```

No extra code needed — `optparse` generates this for free.

## Positional Arguments with `optparse`

When you want to accept an arbitrary number of files (e.g., `*.fasta`):

```r
opt_parser <- OptionParser(
  usage = "Usage: %prog [options] file1 file2 ...",
  option_list = option_list
)

# positional_arguments = TRUE returns both options and extra args
args <- parse_args(opt_parser, positional_arguments = TRUE)

opt         <- args$options   # named options (--outbase etc.)
input_files <- args$args      # positional file names
```

Usage:
```bash
./compare_assemblies.R --outbase results/ asm1.fai asm2.fai asm3.fai
# or with a wildcard:
./compare_assemblies.R --outbase results/ data/assemblies/*.fai
```

## `commandArgs` vs `optparse`

| Feature | `commandArgs` | `optparse` |
|---|---|---|
| Built-in (no install) | yes | no |
| Named flags (`--flag`) | no | yes |
| Auto help message | no | yes |
| Default values | manual | built-in |
| Positional arguments | by position | supported |
| Best for | simple scripts | production scripts |

## Writing Output Files

Use the `--outbase` pattern to produce multiple related outputs:

```r
outbase <- opt$outbase

# Save CSV summary
write.csv(summary_df,
          file = paste0(outbase, "_summary.csv"),
          row.names = FALSE)

# Save PNG plot
png(paste0(outbase, "_plot.png"), width = 800, height = 600)
# ... plotting code ...
dev.off()

cat("Results written to", outbase, "_summary.csv and _plot.png\n")
```

## Example: `compare_assemblies.R` Overview

The script `command_line_scripts/compare_assemblies.R`:

1. Parses `--g1`, `--g2`, `--outbase` with `optparse`
2. Reads two `.fai` FASTA index files
3. Computes **N50**, **L50**, and **total length** for each
4. Saves a CSV summary table
5. Saves a PNG plot of cumulative contig lengths

```bash
./compare_assemblies.R \
  --g1 data/assemblies/asm1.fasta.fai \
  --g2 data/assemblies/asm2.fasta.fai \
  --outbase results/comparison
```

## N50 and L50 — a Reminder

Given contig lengths sorted descending: `[1000, 800, 600, 400, 200]`
Total = 3000, 50% threshold = 1500

| Contigs | Cumulative |
|---|---|
| 1000 | 1000 |
| 800  | 1800 ← passes 1500 |

- **N50** = 800 (length of the contig that tips the balance)
- **L50** = 2 (number of contigs needed)

Better assembly → higher N50, lower L50

## Printing Progress Messages

Use `cat()` or `message()` to keep users informed:

```r
cat("Reading", input_file, "...\n")
# ... processing ...
cat("Wrote results to", output_file, "\n")
```

`message()` prints to **stderr** (separate from stdout — useful in pipelines):

```r
message("Warning: only one contig found in ", basename(fai_file))
```

## Running Scripts from the Repo Root

Scripts in this course expect to be run from the **repository root**:

```bash
# From the repo root:
Rscript 08_RMarkdown_CLI/command_line_scripts/calculate_N50_L50.R \
  data/assemblies/asm1.fasta.fai \
  results/asm1_stats.txt
```

Paths like `data/assemblies/` are relative to the working directory. Running from a different location will break them.

## Summary

- Use `commandArgs(trailingOnly = TRUE)` for simple, positional arguments
- Use `optparse` for named flags, defaults, and auto-generated help
- Use `positional_arguments = TRUE` to accept variable numbers of files
- Use `paste0(outbase, "_suffix.ext")` for output file naming
- Always validate inputs and print helpful error messages
- Scripts are run from the **repository root**

> 📝 **Task 1** — Run `calculate_N50_L50.R` from the command line with `--help`. Inspect the usage message and run it on a provided `.fai` file.

> 📝 **Task 2** — Inspect `compare_assemblies.R`. Add the `--help` flag check and run it on two `.fai` files. Examine the CSV and PNG outputs.
